title: Read your first 5 Thai signs by the end of this video — the alphabet isn't as scary as it looks

vocab.explanation: ห้องน้ำ literally means 'water room' — ห้อง is 'room' and น้ำ is 'water'. This is the word you will see on signs absolutely everywhere in Thailand: shopping malls, restaurants, petrol stations, temples. If you can recognise this one word in Thai script, you will never have to mime your way to the bathroom again. It is the single most important sign a visitor needs to know.

exampleSentences.thai: ห้องน้ำอยู่ชั้นสองค่ะ
exampleSentences.translit: hông náam yùu chán sǎawng khâ
exampleSentences.english: The bathroom is on the second floor.
exampleSentences.note: yùu means 'is located', chán sǎawng means 'second floor'. Notice ห้องน้ำ comes first — the thing you are asking about goes at the start of the sentence.

vocab.explanation: เปิด means 'open', and you will see it on shop doors, restaurant entrances, and gates all over Thailand. It also means 'to turn on' in everyday speech — you can เปิด the lights, เปิด the air conditioning, เปิด the TV. But on a door sign, it always means the place is open for business. One syllable, easy to spot once you know it.

exampleSentences.thai: ร้านนี้เปิดกี่โมง
exampleSentences.english: What time does this shop open?
exampleSentences.note: กี่โมง means 'what time'. เปิด here is used as a verb. Very useful if you are trying to time a visit.

vocab.explanation: ปิด is the opposite of เปิด — it means 'closed'. The two words look very similar in Thai script, and they should, because they are a natural pair. The key difference is tone: เปิด has a falling tone and ปิด has a low tone. In practice on signs, the context tells you everything — if you are standing at a shop door, you will instantly know which one you are looking at. These two together are everything: open or closed.

exampleSentences.thai: ร้านปิดแล้วค่ะ
exampleSentences.note: แล้ว (láaew) means 'already', signalling a completed state. You will hear this from staff if you arrive just after closing time.

vocab.explanation: ทางออก means 'exit' — ทาง means 'path' or 'way', and ออก means 'out'. Literally it is 'way out'. You will see this in every mall, airport, cinema, and hotel lobby in Thailand. The green illuminated exit sign almost always has ทางออก printed on it. Its companion is ทางเข้า — 'entrance' — which uses เข้า, our next word. Learn ทาง and you unlock a whole family of direction signs.

exampleSentences.thai: ทางออกอยู่ทางขวาค่ะ
exampleSentences.translit: thaang àawk yùu thaang khwǎa khâ
exampleSentences.english: The exit is on the right.
exampleSentences.note: ขวา (khwǎa) means 'right side'. Notice ทาง appears twice here: once as part of ทางออก and once on its own meaning 'direction'.

vocab.explanation: เข้า means 'to enter' or 'in'. On signs you will most often see it as ทางเข้า — 'entrance' — which pairs ทาง (path) with เข้า (enter). But เข้า also appears alone on doors and gates to mark the entry point. What I love about this word is that it appears inside other everyday words too: เข้าใจ means 'to understand', and literally it means 'enter the heart'. Thai words are often pictures of their concept like that.

exampleSentences.thai: เข้ามาได้เลยค่ะ
exampleSentences.note: มา means 'come', so เข้ามา together means 'come in'. ได้เลย means 'go right ahead'. You will hear this when a shopkeeper or host waves you inside.

vocab.thaiSplit: ร้าน อาหาร
vocab.explanation: ร้านอาหาร means 'restaurant': ร้าน means 'shop' or 'store', and อาหาร means 'food'. So a restaurant is literally a 'food shop'. What is really useful here is that ร้าน appears at the start of a whole category of shop signs — ร้านกาแฟ is a coffee shop, ร้านยา is a pharmacy, ร้านเสริมสวย is a beauty salon. Once you can recognise ร้าน, you can identify any type of shop even before you read the second word.

exampleSentences.thai: ร้านอาหารนี้อร่อยมากค่ะ
exampleSentences.translit: ráan aa-hǎan níi a-ràai mâak khâ
exampleSentences.note: อร่อย (a-ràai) means 'delicious' — one of the most important words in the Thai language — and มาก means 'very'.

vocab.explanation: ขาย means 'to sell' or 'for sale'. When you see it on a sign, someone is selling something. In Thai markets and at street stalls you will often see handwritten signs where ขาย is followed directly by the item: ขายก๋วยเตี๋ยว means 'noodles for sale', ขายผลไม้ means 'fruit for sale'. Once you can recognise ขาย, you know you are at a vendor — even if you cannot yet read what is being sold.

exampleSentences.thai: ป้ายนั้นเขียนว่าขายผลไม้
exampleSentences.translit: pâai nán khǐan wâa khǎai phǒn-la-máai
exampleSentences.english: That sign says fruit for sale.
exampleSentences.note: ผลไม้ (phǒn-la-máai) means 'fruit'. เขียนว่า literally means 'written as' — a very useful phrase when you are pointing at signs and asking what they say.

vocab.explanation: Here is the surprise of this lesson: ฟรี is a loanword borrowed directly from English, and it is pronounced almost exactly like 'free'. Thai has borrowed hundreds of words this way, especially for modern concepts. If you see ฟรี on a sign — next to free samples at 7-Eleven, free parking, free Wi-Fi — you already know what it means. This is one of dozens of English loanwords hiding inside Thai signs, and once you know they exist, you start spotting them everywhere.

exampleSentences.note: Exactly as you would expect. Thai menus and signs are full of loanwords like this one — โปรโมชั่น (promotion), แอร์ (air conditioning), เซอร์วิส (service).

blocks.speakerNote: A = tourist (Nine wearing a cap and light jacket, looking around at imaginary signs, slightly overwhelmed). B = helpful local (Nine in casual everyday clothes, relaxed and warm, slightly brighter lighting on the right side of the split frame). Voice: A speaks with hesitation and exaggerated uncertainty; B speaks at natural speed with calm confidence. Recording note: This block is the opening clip — record it as a continuous take that can be lifted as a 30-second short.

lines.english: A tourist is surrounded by Thai signs. She has no idea what any of them say.
lines.speaker: narrator

lines.thai: โอ้โห ป้ายเต็มไปหมดเลย อ่านไม่ออกเลยสักคำ
lines.translit: ôo-hǒo pâai tem pai mòt loei àan mâi àawk loei sàk kham
lines.english: Oh wow, signs everywhere. I can't read a single word.
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: อ่านออกแน่นอน ลองดูสิ
lines.translit: àan àawk nâae-nawn laawng duu sì
lines.english: Yes you can. Just try.
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: ป้ายนี้... อ่านว่าอะไรอะ
lines.translit: pâai níi... àan wâa a-rai à
lines.english: This sign... what does it even say?
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: อ่านดูก่อนสิ ไม่ยากเลย
lines.translit: àan duu gàawn sì mâi yâak loei
lines.english: Just try reading it. It's really not hard.
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: ห้อง... ห้องน้ำ!
lines.english: Bathroom!
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: เห็นไหม อ่านออกแล้ว
lines.english: See? You can read it already.
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: เปิดอยู่ไหม... เปิด!
lines.english: Is it open... Open!
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: สองคำแล้วนะ
lines.english: That's two words already.
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: โห ไม่น่าเชื่อเลย
lines.english: Wow, I genuinely can't believe that.
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: ภาษาไทยไม่ได้ยากอย่างที่คิดหรอก
lines.translit: phaa-sǎa thai mâi dâai yâak yàang thîi khít ràawk
lines.english: Thai isn't as hard as you think.
lines.speaker: B

blocks.speakerNote: Cut to Nine in teacher mode, direct to camera. Default cultural context: the English loanword angle. Three alternative openers: (1) Statistic opener: 'Thai script has 44 consonants — I know, that sounds like a lot. But to read the signs you actually use every day in Thailand, you only need to recognise around 15 to 20 of them. Today you are going to learn 8.' (2) Linguistic puzzle opener: 'เปิด and ปิด look almost identical in Thai script — open and closed. One tone separates them. Once you know that difference, you will never confuse them on a door sign again.' (3) Sensory scene opener: 'The first thing most visitors notice walking into a Thai shopping mall is that the signs look completely unreadable. But look again — those signs use a very small set of recurring words, and you are about to learn the most important ones.'

lines.english: That sketch? She was completely frozen looking at Thai signs — but within 30 seconds she could already read two of them. And by the end of this video, you will be able to read eight.

Here is something most people do not realise about Thai: it has borrowed hundreds of words directly from English. You will find them on signs all over the country — ฟรี means 'free', โปรโมชั่น means 'promotion', แอร์ means 'air conditioning'. They are written in Thai script, but they sound almost exactly like the English originals. So some signs you can actually read before you have even learned a single Thai word. That is our secret shortcut today.
By the end of this video, you will be able to read eight real Thai signs in Thai script — bathroom, open, closed, exit, entrance, restaurant, for sale, and free — all in Thai.

I am going to teach you each word one by one, then we will hear them all together in a short conversation, break it down sentence by sentence, and finally you will practise saying them yourself. Let's go.

Let's start with the vocabulary. I am going to go through each word one by one. For each one, I will show you the Thai script, explain what it means, and show you how it looks on a real sign. Ready? Let's go.

lines.thai: ห้องน้ำ

lines.english: Our first word is ห้องน้ำ — hông náam. This means 'bathroom' or 'toilet'. Literally it is 'water room' — ห้อง is 'room' and น้ำ is 'water'. You will see this on signs absolutely everywhere in Thailand: shopping malls, restaurants, petrol stations, temples, anywhere. If you learn nothing else from this video, learn this one. It is the most important sign you will ever need to read.

lines.thai: ห้องน้ำอยู่ชั้นสองค่ะ
lines.translit: hông náam yùu chán sǎawng khâ

lines.english: That means 'The bathroom is on the second floor.' อยู่ means 'is located', ชั้นสอง means 'second floor'. But honestly — if you spot ห้องน้ำ on any sign anywhere in Thailand, that is all the information you need.
OK, next word.

lines.thai: เปิด

lines.english: เปิด — pèrt — means 'open'. You will see this on shop doors all over Thailand. One useful thing to know: it also means 'to turn on' in everyday speech — you can เปิด the lights, เปิด the air conditioning, เปิด the television. But on a door sign, it always means the place is open for business. Short word, one syllable, and once you can recognise those characters, it jumps out at you immediately.

lines.thai: ร้านนี้เปิดกี่โมง

lines.english: That means 'What time does this shop open?' กี่โมง means 'what time'. A very useful question if you are not sure whether somewhere is open yet.
Now the opposite.

lines.thai: ปิด

lines.english: ปิด — pìt — means 'closed'. Now here is the interesting thing: เปิด and ปิด look very similar in Thai script — and they should, because they are opposites that naturally go together. The key difference is tone: เปิด has a falling tone, ปิด has a low tone. In practice on a sign, context tells you everything — if you are at a shop door, you will know immediately which one you are reading, and whether you are getting in or turning around.

lines.thai: ร้านปิดแล้วค่ะ

lines.english: That means 'The shop is already closed.' แล้ว — láaew — means 'already', signalling something is in a completed state. You will hear this a lot if you arrive somewhere just after closing time.
This next one is two syllables — and you will see it in every mall and airport.

lines.thai: ทางออก

lines.english: ทางออก — thaang àawk — means 'exit'. ทาง means 'path' or 'way', and ออก means 'out'. So literally it is 'way out'. You will see this in every shopping mall, airport, cinema, and hotel lobby in Thailand. The green illuminated exit sign almost always has ทางออก printed on it. Its companion word is ทางเข้า — entrance — which uses เข้า, our next word. Once you know ทาง, you unlock a whole family of direction signs.

lines.thai: ทางออกอยู่ทางขวาค่ะ
lines.translit: thaang àawk yùu thaang khwǎa khâ

lines.english: That means 'The exit is on the right.' ขวา means 'right side'. Notice ทาง appears twice: once as part of ทางออก, and once on its own meaning 'direction'.
And the companion word.

lines.thai: เข้า

lines.english: เข้า — khâo — means 'to enter' or 'in'. On signs you will most often see it as ทางเข้า, meaning 'entrance', which pairs ทาง with เข้า. But เข้า also appears alone on doors and gates to mark where you go in. What I love about this word is that it appears inside other everyday words too: เข้าใจ means 'to understand', and literally it means 'enter the heart'. Thai often works like that — words are pictures of their meaning.

lines.thai: เข้ามาได้เลยค่ะ

lines.english: That means 'Come on in, please.' มา means 'come', so เข้ามา together means 'come in'. ได้เลย means 'go right ahead'. You will hear this when a shopkeeper or host waves you inside.
This next one is one you will really need.

lines.thai: ร้านอาหาร

lines.english: ร้านอาหาร — ráan aa-hǎan — means 'restaurant'. It is a two-part word: ร้าน means 'shop', and อาหาร means 'food'. So a restaurant is literally a 'food shop'. What is really powerful to know is that ร้าน appears at the front of a whole category of signs: ร้านกาแฟ is a coffee shop, ร้านยา is a pharmacy, ร้านเสริมสวย is a beauty salon. Once you can recognise ร้าน, you can identify the type of any shop on the street.

lines.thai: ร้านอาหารนี้อร่อยมากค่ะ
lines.translit: ráan aa-hǎan níi a-ràai mâak khâ

lines.english: That means 'This restaurant is very delicious.' อร่อย means 'delicious' — one of the most important words in the Thai language — and มาก means 'very'. You will use this sentence a lot.
OK, two more. This one you will see a lot in markets.

lines.thai: ขาย

lines.english: ขาย — khǎai — means 'to sell' or 'for sale'. When you see it on a sign, something is being sold. In Thai markets and at street stalls you will often see handwritten signs where ขาย is followed directly by the item: ขายก๋วยเตี๋ยว is noodles for sale, ขายผลไม้ is fruit for sale. Even if you cannot yet read what follows, spotting ขาย tells you straight away that this is a vendor — which is often all you need to know.

lines.thai: ป้ายนั้นเขียนว่าขายผลไม้
lines.translit: pâai nán khǐan wâa khǎai phǒn-la-máai

lines.english: That means 'That sign says fruit for sale.' ผลไม้ means 'fruit'. เขียนว่า literally means 'written as' — it is a useful phrase when you are pointing at a sign and saying what it says.
And our last word — and this one is going to surprise you.

lines.thai: ฟรี

lines.english: ฟรี — frii — means 'free', as in no charge. And here is why this word is special: it is a loanword borrowed directly from English, and it sounds almost exactly like 'free'. Thai has taken in hundreds of words like this — especially for modern, imported ideas. You will see ฟรี on signs for free samples at 7-Eleven, free car parking, free Wi-Fi. And if you spot it on a sign today, you already understand it, because you already know English. This is the secret hiding inside Thai signs that most beginners walk straight past.

lines.thai: Wi-Fi ฟรีค่ะ

lines.english: Wi-Fi ฟรี — the Wi-Fi is free. Exactly as you would expect. This kind of sign is everywhere in Thai cafes and restaurants, and now you can read it in Thai script.

OK, so now you know all eight words. What I am going to do is put them into a short conversation — imagine you are walking around a Thai shopping mall with a local friend, and you are pointing at signs together. I am going to speak at natural speed, the way a Thai person would actually talk. Do not pause the video. Just listen and see how much you can catch. Ready? Here we go.

lines.thai: ห้องน้ำอยู่ที่ไหนคะ
ดูป้ายนั้นสิ เขียนว่าห้องน้ำ
ร้านนี้เปิดหรือยังคะ
เปิดแล้วค่ะ ป้ายบอกว่าเปิด
ทางออกอยู่ที่ไหนคะ
ป้ายทางออกอยู่ตรงนั้นเลยค่ะ

lines.english: How was that? Could you catch some of those words? Don't worry if you did not get everything — that is completely normal at this stage. Now let's go through it sentence by sentence so you can see exactly what was said.
I am going to go through that same conversation again, but this time sentence by sentence. I will say the Thai, and then explain exactly what each part means.

lines.thai: ห้องน้ำอยู่ที่ไหนคะ

lines.english: Where is the bathroom?

ห้องน้ำ — bathroom. อยู่ — is located. ที่ไหน — where. คะ — the polite question particle a woman uses. So the word order is: the thing you are asking about, then the location verb, then the question word, then politeness. In English you say 'WHERE is the bathroom' — where goes first. In Thai, the question word ที่ไหน goes at the end. That is a pattern that runs through almost every Thai question, and once you notice it, it will not surprise you again.

lines.thai: ดูป้ายนั้นสิ เขียนว่าห้องน้ำ
lines.translit: duu pâai nán sì khǐan wâa hông náam
duu pâai nán sì khǐan wâa hông náam

lines.english: Look at that sign — it says bathroom.

ดู means 'look at'. ป้าย means 'sign'. นั้น means 'that'. สิ is a little particle that makes the sentence a gentle suggestion — like 'go on, have a look'. เขียนว่า means 'written as' or 'it says' — literally 'written that'. This is one of the most useful phrases when you are pointing at a sign you want to read: เขียนว่าอะไร means 'what does it say?'

lines.thai: ร้านนี้เปิดหรือยังคะ
lines.translit: ráan níi pèrt rǔu yang khá
ráan níi pèrt rǔu yang khá

lines.english: Is this shop open yet?

ร้านนี้ — this shop. เปิด — open. หรือยัง — literally 'or yet?' — the Thai way of asking whether something has happened yet. หรือ on its own means 'or', and ยัง means 'yet'. So หรือยัง at the end of a sentence is asking: has this happened yet, or not? It is a very common spoken pattern and sounds completely natural to a Thai ear.

lines.thai: เปิดแล้วค่ะ ป้ายบอกว่าเปิด
lines.translit: pèrt láaew khâ pâai bàawk wâa pèrt
pèrt láaew khâ pâai bàawk wâa pèrt

lines.english: It's open — the sign says open.

เปิดแล้ว — open already, meaning it has opened. แล้ว signals that something is in a completed state — it has happened. ป้ายบอกว่า means 'the sign says' — บอก means 'to tell' or 'to say'. Notice เปิด appears twice in this sentence: first as the answer, then as the word the sign actually shows. A nice bit of confirmation.

lines.thai: ทางออกอยู่ที่ไหนคะ

lines.english: Where is the exit?

This sentence follows exactly the same frame as sentence one: thing + อยู่ + ที่ไหน + politeness particle. Once you learn this pattern, you can ask 'where is anything' in Thai — just swap the first word. ห้องน้ำอยู่ที่ไหน — where is the bathroom. ร้านอาหารอยู่ที่ไหน — where is the restaurant. The structure stays identical. That is one pattern doing a lot of work.

lines.thai: ป้ายทางออกอยู่ตรงนั้นเลยค่ะ
lines.translit: pâai thaang àawk yùu trong nán loei khâ
pâai thaang àawk yùu trong nán loei khâ

lines.english: The exit sign is right there.

ป้ายทางออก — the exit sign. อยู่ — is located. ตรงนั้น — right there, literally 'straight that direction'. เลย is a particle that adds emphasis: 'right there, just there'. ค่ะ — polite ending. You will notice เลย keeps appearing in these sentences as an emphasiser — it is worth getting comfortable with because Thai people use it constantly in natural speech.

Now it is your turn to practise. I am going to say each sentence slowly, and I want you to repeat after me. Don't be shy — say it out loud! Speaking is how you learn. I will leave a gap after each sentence for you to repeat.

lines.thai: ห้องน้ำอยู่ที่ไหนคะ
lines.thaiSplit: ห้องน้ำ อยู่ ที่ไหน คะ

lines.thai: ดูป้ายนั้นสิ เขียนว่าห้องน้ำ
lines.thaiSplit: ดู ป้าย นั้น สิ เขียน ว่า ห้องน้ำ
lines.translit: duu pâai nán sì khǐan wâa hông náam

lines.thai: ร้านนี้เปิดหรือยังคะ
lines.thaiSplit: ร้าน นี้ เปิด หรือ ยัง คะ
lines.translit: ráan níi pèrt rǔu yang khá

lines.thai: เปิดแล้วค่ะ ป้ายบอกว่าเปิด
lines.thaiSplit: เปิด แล้ว ค่ะ ป้าย บอก ว่า เปิด
lines.translit: pèrt láaew khâ pâai bàawk wâa pèrt

lines.thai: ทางออกอยู่ที่ไหนคะ
lines.thaiSplit: ทางออก อยู่ ที่ไหน คะ

lines.thai: ป้ายทางออกอยู่ตรงนั้นเลยค่ะ
lines.thaiSplit: ป้าย ทางออก อยู่ ตรง นั้น เลย ค่ะ
lines.translit: pâai thaang àawk yùu trong nán loei khâ

lines.english: Great job! If that felt hard, that is completely fine — rewind and do it again. Repetition is how this sticks. Every time you say these sentences out loud, they get a little more natural.
OK, quiz time! I am going to give you a situation in English, and you need to say the Thai phrase. I will give you a few seconds to think, and then I will give you the answer. Ready?

blocks.speakerNote: Look to camera. Ask the question, then hold 3 seconds of silence.

lines.english: You are in a Thai shopping mall and you urgently need to find the bathroom. What sign are you looking for?
Try saying it now...

lines.thai: ห้องน้ำ

lines.english: That is right — ห้องน้ำ, hông náam. Two syllables. If you can spot those characters on a sign in Thailand, you are set.
You walk up to a shop door and see the word ปิด. What does it mean — and should you try to go in?
Try saying it now...

blocks.speakerNote: Shake head with a slight smile.

lines.thai: ปิด

lines.english: That is right — ปิด, pìt. It means 'closed'. The shop is closed. Do not try the door. Now you know.
You are trying to leave a Thai cinema and you need to find the way out. What sign are you looking for?
Try saying it now...

blocks.speakerNote: Point slightly to the side as if gesturing toward a sign.

lines.thai: ทางออก

lines.english: That is right — ทางออก, thaang àawk. It means 'exit', the way out. ทาง is path, ออก is out. Follow the ทางออก sign.

blocks.speakerNote: Small smile before asking — this is the fun one.

lines.english: You are at a 7-Eleven and you see a tag next to some snack samples that says ฟรี. What does it mean?
Try saying it now...

lines.thai: ฟรี

lines.english: That is right — ฟรี, frii. It means 'free'. And yes, it sounds almost exactly like the English word. Go ahead and grab the snack.
Almost done! You have just learned eight real Thai signs. Let's make sure they all stick.
Let's do a quick recap of everything you learned today.

Eight signs: ห้องน้ำ bathroom, เปิด open, ปิด closed, ทางออก exit, เข้า entrance, ร้านอาหาร restaurant, ขาย for sale, and ฟรี free. Next time you are in Thailand, look up at any sign and see how many of those you can spot. I think you will surprise yourself. Thanks for watching — if this helped you, give it a thumbs up and subscribe so you don't miss the next one.

blocks.speakerNote: Pre-Rev-6 — may need retro-fit when template updates. Thematic tease: asking prices is the natural next step after reading signs in shops and restaurants.

lines.english: Now that you can find the restaurant and walk through the entrance — next time, we are going to learn how to ask how much something costs.

lines.thai: ราคาเท่าไหร่
lines.english: How much does it cost?
